coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanograss for supercapacitors
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Perumal, Suguna
Thirukumaran, Periyasamy
Vinodh, Rajangam
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, a novel N-doped carbon nanograss (NCNG) were synthesized from the used baby-diaper absorbent via carbonization under the argon atmosphere and has been applied as an electroactive material for flexible supercapacitor. The synthesized NCNG display a grass-like morphology with an acceptable degree of graphitization. The surface areas and average pore size of NCNG were 183 m2 g−1 and 3.3 nm, respectively. On the basis of good structural ordering with reasonable surface area, the NCNG was coated on a carbon cloth and studied their electrochemical performance toward the supercapacitors. The obtained quasi-rectangular shaped cyclic voltammetry curves indicate good capacitive behavior of the NCNG. The galvanostatic charge-discharge experimental results showed that the specific capacitance of NCNG was 81 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 with capacitance retention of 95% after 10,000 cycles of charge-discharge. The slow decrements of specific capacitance with the increment of current density indicate superior rate capability of synthesized NCNG. These excellent electrochemical performances of the synthesized NCNG from used (waste) baby-diaper extend their potential applications in various nanoelectronics field. To the best of authors knowledge, first time the NCNG synthesized from the garbage-waste and can extend their synthesis in large-scale.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Used baby-diaper
Waste utilization
Carbonization
N-doped carbon nanograss
Supercapacitor

Characterization of the synthesized N-doped carbon nanograss
Electrochemical performances of the synthesized N-doped carbon nanograss for supercapacitors
Morphological study of the fabricated working electrode

Also, the author greatly acknowledge the National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education , Science, and Technology (2012M3A7B4049675) and the Priority Research Centers Program (2014R1A6A1031189).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–e) FESEM images of the synthesized N-doped carbon nanograss with different magnifications and inset (e) photograph of natural grass.
(a) FESEM EDS image of the synthesized N-doped carbon nanograss with the corresponding elemental mapping of (b) carbon, (c) oxygen and (d) nitrogen.
TEM images of the synthesized N-doped carbon nanograss with different magnifications.
(a) Raman spectrum, (b) nitrogen physisorption isotherms, (c) pore size distribution graph and (d) ATR-FTIR spectrum of the synthesized N-doped carbon nanograss.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of the synthesized N-doped carbon nanograss, the XPS high-resolution spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) O 1s region of the synthesized N-doped carbon nanograss.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms at different scan rates, (b) galvanostatic charge–discharge curves at different current densities, (c) specific capacitances at different current densities, and (d) capacitance retention vs. cycle number measured at a current density of 5 A g−1 of the synthesized N-doped carbon nanograss.

Electrochemical impedance spectrum (a) Nyquist plot in the frequency range of 10 mHz–10 kHz with an alternate current amplitude of 10 mV, Inset of (a) equivalent electric circuit model and Nyquist plot in the high-frequency region of (a), (b) frequency dependent amplitude of Bode plot and (c) frequency dependent phase angles of Bode plot of the synthesized N-doped carbon nanograss. (d) Nyquist plots of the synthesized N-doped carbon nanograss before and after cycles (5000 and 10,000 GCD). Inset (d) Nyquist plots in the high-frequency region of (d).

(a) Cyclic voltammograms at different scan rates, (b) galvanostatic charge-discharge curves at different current densities, (c) gravimetric specific capacitances at different current densities, and (d) Ragone plots.

FESEM images of the fabricated working electrode (NCNG coated carbon cloth) (a-c) before electrochemical measurements, (d–f) after 5000 GCD cycles and (g-i) after 10,000 GCD cycles of electrochemical measurements.
Synthesis procedure of the N-doped carbon nanograss from the used baby-diaper.
Comparison of the specific capacitances of the synthesized NCNG electroactive material with the recently reported carbonaceous materials.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanograss for supercapacitors

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, a novel N-doped carbon nanograss (NCNG) were synthesized from the used baby-diaper absorbent via carbonization under the argon atmosphere and has been applied as an electroactive material for flexible supercapacitor. The synthesized NCNG display a grass-like morphology with an acceptable degree of graphitization. The surface areas and average pore size of NCNG were 183 m2 g−1 and 3.3 nm, respectively. On the basis of good structural ordering with reasonable surface area, the NCNG was coated on a carbon cloth and studied their electrochemical performance toward the supercapacitors. The obtained quasi-rectangular shaped cyclic voltammetry curves indicate good capacitive behavior of the NCNG. The galvanostatic charge-discharge experimental results showed that the specific capacitance of NCNG was 81 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 with capacitance retention of 95% after 10,000 cycles of charge-discharge. The slow decrements of specific capacitance with the increment of current density indicate superior rate capability of synthesized NCNG. These excellent electrochemical performances of the synthesized NCNG from used (waste) baby-diaper extend their potential applications in various nanoelectronics field. To the best of authors knowledge, first time the NCNG synthesized from the garbage-waste and can extend their synthesis in large-scale.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, renewable and eco-friendly energy sources are intensively consumed from fossil fuel usage which are linked with environmental pollution. For this, an urgent new development of sustainable energy and storage systems are needed. Supercapacitors are one of the important class of environmentally-friendly electrochemical energy storage device. They have promising attention in the areas of electric vehicles, backup power systems, and consumer electronics owing to their high power density, extremely long cycle life, and fast charge-discharge characteristics [1–9]. On the basis of energy storage (capacitive) mechanism, supercapacitors are divided into three classifications as follows [10]: (i) electrical double-layer capacitors (EDLC) based on ion adsorption in which physically stores charges through reversible electrostatic attraction at the electrode-electrolyte interface without any electron transfer process between the electrode and electrolyte. This type supercapacitor is inexpensive, long cycle life, high electron transport of the electrode material but show low capacitance of electrode. Carbon structures are the main source of EDLC electrode active materials. (ii) Faradaic pseudocapacitors in which the charge storage occur through the fast and reversible electrochemical redox reactions at the electrode material surface with electron transfer between the electrolyte and electrode. The pseudocapacitor possesses the character of high capacitance, low electron transport of electrode and relative poor life cycle due to the volume transformation during the Faradaic reaction process. Most commonly used electroactive materials in Faradaic pseudocapacitors are metal oxides, metal hydroxides, metal sulfides, metal nitrides, metal carbides and conducting polymers [11]. (iii) In hybrid supercapacitors, one cell contains two electrodes, the EDLC electrode, and the battery electrode. In general, carbon-based materials, conducting polymers and metal oxides/hydroxides are the three main electrode materials used in supercapacitors. Among them, carbon-based materials are ideal for supercapacitors owing to a large surface area and possess reasonable specific capacitance with long lifespan [12].

In recent years, flexible supercapacitors have rapid attention from researchers because of their distinct advantages flexibility, easy handling and so on. Mechanical property and energy density are important parameters for flexible supercapacitors. In order to get good mechanical strength for the working electrode, the active electrode materials have been loaded on the electrochemically active substrate including carbon cloth, graphite paper, graphene foam, carbon fiber thread and polyethylene terephthalate [13]. Among them, carbon cloth is an economical substrate. For the effective flexible supercapacitors, porosity with the surface area of the active electrode materials must be as high as possible.

In the present study, new types of mesoporous N-doped carbon nanograss (NCNG) with a reasonable surface area was synthesized for flexible supercapacitor. The NCNG was synthesized from used baby-diaper (garbage collected Huggies) by simple carbonization at 800 °C under the argon atmosphere. The morphology, elemental composition, and surface functional groups of synthesized NCNG were investigated extensively by various analytical techniques. The working electrode was fabricated using the resulted NCNG as an electroactive material and carbon cloth as an electrochemically active substrate. All the electrochemical measurements including cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed for fabricated working electrode towards supercapacitors. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the synthesis of NCNG from used baby-diaper absorbent and applied as an electroactive material for flexible supercapacitors. Furthermore, the electrochemical activities of NCNG for supercapacitors are discussed.

Used baby-diaper (urinated baby-diaper) was collected from the garbage, Gyeongsan, Republic of Korea. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Republic of Korea. Carbon cloth was purchased from FuelCellsEtc (Commercial Fuel Cell Components Manufacturer). Argon gas was purchased from Dukson, Republic of Korea. All of the chemicals were used as received and deionized water was used throughout this work.

Absorbent was collected from the used baby-diaper (only urine absorbed diapers) with wet-condition and was dried at 100 °C in a hot air oven for overnight. The dried material was calcined (carbonized) at 800 °C in a tubular furnace under the argon atmosphere. The synthesized black powder was ground well to make a homogeneous mixture by using agate mortar and pestle (Scheme 1 ). The resulted fine powder was characterized extensively by various physicochemical techniques. On the basis of the structural and elemental analysis, the obtained material denoted as N-doped carbon nanograss (NCNG) and was used as an electroactive material for the fabrication of flexible supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrode fabrication and electrochemical measurements of the synthesized N-doped carbon nanograss
ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesized NCNG was used as an active electrode material for the fabrication of the working electrode. The typical procedure for the fabrication of the working electrode as follows: NCNG and PVDF were ground well with a ratio of 85:15 in NMP to make a fine suspension. A simple drop-casting method was used to coat the electroactive material (NCNG)-containing fine suspensions on an electrochemically active substrate (carbon cloth) with an area of 1.0 cm2 and then this working electrode was dried at 100 °C in a hot air oven overnight and total mass of electroactive material was 3 mg. The electrochemical measurements (capacitive performance) including CV, GCD, and EIS of NCNG was performed in the aqueous solution of 1 M H2SO4 with a three-electrode system under atmospheric condition using the CorrTest-CS350 electrochemical workstation. The NCNG coated carbon cloth, Ag/AgCl and platinum plate (1 cm2) were employed as working, reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The CV analyses were performed with a potential window of 0.0 − 1.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) under the scan rates from 10 to 100 mV s−1. The GCD measurements were performed with a potential window of 0.0 − 1.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at the current densities varied from 0.5 to 10 A g−1. Alternating current amplitude of 10 mV with the frequency from 10 mHz to 10 kHz was chosen for EIS measurements. The specific capacitances (Cs ) of the electroactive materials were obtained from their GCD curves according to the following Eq. (1) [14].
(1)
C s
=
I
Δ
t
m
Δ
V
whereas, I is the current in the charge-discharge process (A), Δt represents to the discharge time (s), ΔV stands for the potential window during the charge-discharge measurement (V) and m donates the mass of the electroactive materials (g).

For the two-electrode system, the total mass of active material was maintained to be 6 mg in the assembled cell. The electrochemical performance of two electrode symmetric supercapacitor was evaluated by CV and GCD methods. The gravimetric specific capacitances (Cgs ), energy density (ED ) and power density (PD ) of two electrode device were calculated using the following Eqs. (2)–(4) [15].
(2)
C
g s
= 2
I
Δ
t
m
Δ
V
(3)
E D
=
C
g s
Δ
V 2
2 × 3.6
(4)
P D
= 3600 ×
E D
Δ
t
where Cgs is the gravimetric specific capacitance, △V is the voltage change of the supercapacitor between completely charged and discharged, and △t is the discharge time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the synthesized N-doped carbon nanograss
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the synthesized NCNG was revealed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and transmittance electron microscopy (TEM) analyses. Fig. 1 (a-e) shows the FESEM images of the synthesized NCNG at different magnifications. The FESEM image in Fig. 1(a and b) reveals the dense uniform grass-like array architecture pointed outwards from the skeletons of the carbon. Fig. 1(e) shows that the grass-like carbon is well distributed with sharp edges. The carbon nanograss have a length and diameter of about 10−15 µm and 50−100 nm, respectively. Inset of Fig. 1(e) is the photograph of natural grass which suggests the synthesized carbon materials closely identical to the grass-like morphology. Further, the uniform grass-like carbon grown over the base of porous carbon is clear from low magnification FESEM image (Fig. S1). Inset of Fig. S1 represents the carbon base is highly porous nature which enhances the high surface area and surface area connected with high-performance of a supercapacitor. The elemental compositions of the carbon nanograss were avowed by elemental mapping with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) graph. FESEM image (Fig. 2 (a)) and the corresponding elemental mapping (Fig. 2(b–d)) indicates the synthesized carbon nanograss composition of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements. Also, the elemental mapping results evident the uniform distribution of nitrogen within the carbon nanograss. Based on the results, the synthesized carbon nanograss denoted and confirmed as N-doped carbon nanograss (NCNG). The morphology of NCNG was further confirmed by the TEM analysis. Fig. 3 (a–c) shows the TEM images of the synthesized NCNG at different magnifications. As expected, the images show several grass-like carbons has been grown over the porous carbon base. The carbon nanograss have a diameter of about 50−100 nm with the sharp edges.

Crystallinity and graphitization of the synthesized NCNG were revealed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern and Raman spectrum. Fig. S2 shows the XRD patterns of synthesized NCNG, pure and used (urinated) diaper-absorbent. XRD patterns of the pure diaper-absorbent shows only one broad peak around 23° (2θ) attributed to the polyacrylate, which is generally used to prepare diaper. Normally, baby diaper-absorbent was made of polyacrylate. The XRD pattern of urinated diaper-absorbent displays the distinct peaks apart from the polyacrylate peak. The corresponding materials (peaks) originated from the baby-urine. In general, urine is an aqueous solution of greater than 95% water. Other major constituents include urea, chloride, sodium, and potassium. The XRD pattern of the synthesized NCNG is similar to that of a urinated diaper-absorbent pattern but the intensity of the peaks are higher. The greater intensity due to the calcination at a higher temperature (800 °C) suggests the good crystallinity of the urine components. The broad polyacrylate peak (2θ = 23°) was disappeared. In addition, the sharp carbon peaks emerged around 24 and 42° corresponds to the (002) and (100) planes of typical carbon materials [16] suggest that the polyacrylate was carbonized at 800 °C. The peak intensity of carbon is low and 2θ value is slightly lower than the typical graphitic carbon which might be due to the synthesized NCNG is a low/acceptable degree of crystallization/graphitization [17]. Further, the graphitization of the synthesized NCNG was confirmed by Raman spectrum. The Raman spectrum in Fig. 4 (a) display the characteristic G-band (graphitic band) centered at a frequency of about 1601 cm−1, indicating the consists of sp2-bonded carbon. Though, most of the carbonaceous materials have defects in their graphitic structure, mostly edge of the graphitic structure. This denoted as D-band (defect/disorder band) which is centered at about 1360 cm−1 [18–20]. The defects arising due to the doping of heteroatoms such as nitrogen, oxygen and etc., in the graphitic structure. The intensity of D-band is little higher than that of G-band suggests the synthesized NCNG have a defected graphitic structure that confirms the moderate degree of graphitization, which is consistent with the XRD result. In addition, the broadband centered at about 2700−2900 cm−1 corresponds to 2D- or G′-band which results from the overtone of the D-band [21]. The broad shape suggests multiple layers of graphitic carbon which is consisting of 2-D and 3-D graphitic phases consistent with the FESEM and TEM results.

The nitrogen physisorption (adsorption–desorption) isotherms are the better choice to determine the porosity includes specific surface area and the pore diameter of the synthesized materials. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and their corresponding pore size distribution graph of the synthesized NCNG are displayed in Fig. 4(b) and (c), respectively. The synthesized NCNG yield a type IV with distinct H4 hysteresis loop which implies the existence of mesopores in the sample due to the capillary condensation of molecules in the mesopores [22,23]. The Brunauer–Emmet–Teller (BET) surface area of NCNG was measured as 183 m2 g−1 and their average pore size (diameter) was measured as 3.3 nm. The functional groups of the synthesized NCNG were revealed by attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy analysis. ATR-FTIR spectrum (Fig. 4(d)), displays an absorption band centered at 3320 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibration of NH/OH groups [24, 25]. The OH could be originated from the surface hydroxyl groups and or physically adsorbed water molecules. The absorption bands at 2920 and 2851 cm−1 are characteristic of the CH asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations, respectively [26]. The vibrational bands at 2230, 1740, and 1220 cm−1 owing to the presence of CC/CN, CO, and COH functional units in the graphitic carbon structure. The vibration band of the aromatic CC stretching at 1586 cm−1 indicates the presence of sp2 hybridized honeycomb lattice [27]. Moreover, the two strong absorption bands at 1430 and 1002 cm−1 are attributed to the aromatic CN heterocycles stretching vibrations and COC (epoxy) stretching vibrations, respectively [28]. Fig. S3 shows the FTIR spectra of pure and used diaper-absorbent. The spectra present the corresponding functional groups of polyacrylate and urine. The results indicate that the synthesized NCNG possessed rich oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on their surface during the carbonization.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were further performed to authenticate the chemical composition (functionalization) and bonding environment of the synthesized NCNG. The XPS survey spectrum of NCNG (Fig. 5 (a)) clearly reveals the presence of carbon (C 1s), nitrogen (N 1s) and oxygen (O 1s) in the NCNG at the binding energy level of 284, 400 and 531 eV, respectively. In addition, the major peaks around 1072 and 497 eV can be attributed to Na 1s and Na KLL, respectively which originate from urine which is absorbed in the diaper absorbent. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of NCNG (Fig. 5(b)) further deconvoluted in to six peaks at 283.78, 284.88, 286.08, 287.58, 288.98 and 289.98 eV which can be ascribed to the CC, CC, COH/CN, COC/CNC, CO and OCOH bonds, respectively [29,30]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum of NCNG (Fig. 5(c)) contains three different bonding environments. The peaks centered at 398.18, 399.58, and 400.48 eV are assigned to CNC (pyridinic nitrogen), NH (pyrrolic nitrogen), and (C)3–N (graphitic nitrogen) bonding, respectively [31–33]. This verifies that the synthesized CNG is nitrogen-doped (NCNG) with different environments in the carbon framework which shows good agreements with the FTIR results. The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of NCNG (Fig. 5(d)) depicts distinct three peaks at 530.78, 531.78 and 532.88 eV are associated with oxygen in the states of CO COC/COH, and OCOH, respectively [34–36]. All these results strongly suggest that the synthesized NCNG having significant amount oxygen and nitrogen atoms in the graphitic structure and consists of multiple graphitic carbon layers with mesoporous nature that certainly will enhance the electrochemical behavior of the NCNG discussed below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performances of the synthesized N-doped carbon nanograss for supercapacitors
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical measurements of the synthesized NCNG were carried out using the 1 M H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte with the three-electrode system. Fig. 6 (a) shows the CV curves for the synthesized NCNG electroactive material with the potential range of 0.0–1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl at different potential sweep rate from 10 to 100 mV s–1. All of the CV curves are nearly rectangular in shape without any considerable hump indicate the pure electrical double-layer (EDL) capacitance. The current density increases with increase in the scan rates without any major change of CV curves, indicating that no faradaic reaction happens and the ions are adsorbed on the electrode surface by forming an EDL [37,38]. Fig. 6(b) shows the GCD curves of the synthesized NCNG electroactive material at different current densities of 0.5–10.0 A g–1 with potential windows of 0.0–1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The GCD curves are nearly triangular with symmetric indicating stable capacitance during charging and discharging as well as EDLC behaviour of the electroactive materials. The charge-discharge progressively decreases with increasing applied current density. The specific capacitances of NCNG electroactive material were calculated using Eq. (1) based on the discharge time at various current densities as shown in Fig. 6(c). The specific capacitances were 81, 67, 56, 47, 43, 42 and 40 F g−1 at the current densities of 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 10 A g–1, respectively. Obviously, the specific capacitance gradually decreases with increasing applied current density and it decreases to ∼50% of its initial value due to the ion transport in the porous electrode becomes lethargic at high current density. This concludes the synthesized NCNG electroactive materials exhibit a current density dependent good retention ability. The maximum specific capacitance of 81 F g−1 was obtained at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 for NCNG electroactive materials. The higher specific capacitance of NCNG electroactive materials is due to their novel shape and reasonable BET surface area with homogeneous mesoporosity. The stability of the supercapacitor was evaluated by repeated charging and discharging study. Fig. 6(d) shows the stability of the synthesized NCNG electroactive material at a current density of 5 A g−1 for over 10,000 cycles. The specific capacitance gradually decreased to 95% of the initial value after 10,000 cycles. This insignificant change confirms the electrochemical stability of NCNG for long cycle life. Further, to confirm the stability of the synthesized NCNG electroactive materials, the CV and GCD were measured after the cyclability test (5000 and 10000th cycles) and compared to initial once. Fig. S4 shows the CV curves of 1st, 5000th and 10000th cycles at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 for the synthesized NCNG electroactive materials. The rectangular shape and area of CV curves retain it even after 10,000 cycles indicating stable capacitance due to the mesoporous of the synthesized NCNG. Fig. S5 shows the GCD curves of 1st, 5000th and 10000th cycles at a constant current density of 0.5 A g−1 for the synthesized NCNG electroactive materials. The 10000th cycle of GCD curves shape and discharging time is almost similar to that of the 1st cycle. These studies suggest that the synthesized NCNG electroactive materials possess electrochemically stable with high capacitance retention (%).

EIS measurements were carried out in order to study the electrochemical behaviour including resistive and capacitive components of the supercapacitors. Fig. 7 (a) shows the Nyquist plot for the synthesized NCNG electroactive materials. The intersection of the impedance spectra with the real axis (Z′) at the high-frequency end is the bulk/solution resistance (Rs), which includes the contact resistance and the resistance of the electrolyte. The high-frequency region of the Nyquist plot of electroactive material exhibits a distorted small semicircle corresponds to the charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the contact interface between the electrode and the electrolyte solution and is ascribed to the double-layer capacitance [39–42]. The distorted semicircle clearly saw in the inset of Fig. 7(a) and the diameter of the semicircle represents the electron transfer resistance at the electrode surface [43]. Subsequently, a slanting line at mid-frequency range and a nearly vertical (inclined) line at low-frequency region correspond to the ion-diffusion resistance in the electrolyte and the mass capacitance with leakage resistance in parallel combination [44]. The Nyquist plot is fitted and interpreted with the help of an equivalent electric circuit (R1 + Q2/R2 + Q3/R3) with Z-View software, as shown in the inset of Fig. 7(a). The resistance (R) and capacitance (Q) values are calculated from the EIS measurement which is more clear from the EIS Bode plot of frequency-dependent amplitude (Fig. 7(b)) and frequency-dependent phase angle (Fig. 7(c)). The solution resistance (R1), charge-transfer resistance (R2) and resistance of coating (R3) are calculated as 1.879, 7.182 and 13,984 Ω cm−2, respectively. The capacitances of 0.383 and 0.206 F cm−2 corresponds to the Q2 and Q3, respectively. The Rs and Rct values are considerably lower values and displayed good capacitive values suggesting the NCNG electroactive materials is more suitable for the energy storage device. To demonstrate the superior activity of the synthesized NCNG electroactive materials, the present study was compared with the recent reports of carbonaceous materials and results were shown in Table 1
[45–54]. The present work displays an excellent electrochemical performance than the other reported carbonaceous materials. Hence, the results proving that the synthesized NCNG electroactive materials delivered a high specific capacitance with long-term stability which might be due to the electrochemically stable carbon structure originated from the used diaper-absorbent. Further, to validate the electrochemical stability of the synthesized NCNG electroactive materials, the EIS was measured after cyclability of NCNG and compare to before the cycle test of EIS. Fig. 7(d) shows the EIS Nyquist plots of the synthesized NCNG electroactive material before and after the cyclability test (After 5000 and 10,000 cycles). In the spectra, all the Nyquist plots (before and after 5000, 10,000 cycles) are nearly similar except solution resistance. The solution resistance value little increased for the synthesized NCNG after 5000 and 10,000 cycles. The Nyquist plot of NCNG after 5000 cycles is fitted (Fig. S6) and interpreted with the help of an equivalent electric circuit (R1 + Q2/R2 + Q3/R3) with Z-View software, as shown in the inset of Fig. S6. The solution resistance (R1), charge-transfer resistance (R2) and resistance of coating (R3) are calculated as 1.948, 9.517 and 4034 Ω cm−2, respectively. The capacitances of 0.312 and 0.204 F cm−2 corresponds to the Q2 and Q3, respectively for the NCNG after 5000 cycles. Similarly, the Nyquist plot of NCNG after 10,000 cycles is fitted (Fig. S7) and interpreted with the help of an equivalent electric circuit (R1 + Q2/R2 + Q3/R3) with Z-View software, as shown in the inset of Fig. S7. The solution resistance (R1), charge-transfer resistance (R2) and resistance of coating (R3) are calculated as 1.951, 10.013 and 3585 Ω cm−2, respectively. The capacitances of Q2 and Q3 for the NCNG are calculated as 0.305 and 0.208 F cm−2, respectively for after 10,000 cycles. The values for the NCNG after 10,000 cycles are insignificant increment from the synthesized NCNG electroactive material before the GCD (10,000) cycle test. These results indicating that the synthesized NCNG electroactive material is electrochemically stable in 1 M H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte due to the mesoporous structure of NCNG electroactive material [55]. The mesopores enhance the ion-diffusion between the electrode and electrolyte solution without any structural defect of the NCNG electroactive material. On the basis of the electrochemical properties, the synthesized NCNG is an ideal candidate for supercapacitor with good long-term stability.

In order to show the practical applicability, symmetrical two-electrode cell was fabricated using the synthesized NCNG over carbon cloth. The assembled two-electrode cell was tested in 1 M aqueous H2SO4 using CV and GCD methods. Fig. 8 (a) shows the CV curves of two-electrode cell at different scan rates (10−100 mV S−1) with potential window of 0 − 1.5 V. It is obvious that all the CV curves look like rectangular shapes without any redox-peaks, which confirmed the double layer ideal capacitive response of the synthesized NCNG electroactive material in H2SO4. In addition, the total current area of the CV curves is gradually decreased from fast to slow scan rates. At slow scan rate, the vast number of electrolyte ions access the electrode surface and hence the specific capacity is comparatively higher in slow scan rates. Fig. 8(b) depicts the GCD curves of two-electrode NCNG electroactive material at various current densities such as 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 A g−1. The figure illustrates triangular shaped GCD curves, which exhibit the linear dependence of stored charge under the applied potential window. This result revealed that the charge storage behavior of the assembled two-electrode cell is fully based on the electrostatic accumulation of ions present in the electrolyte on porous double layer structures of the synthesized NCNG electroactive material. Further, the gravimetric specific capacitance of used baby-diaper derived NCNG electroactive material was calculated using the Eq. (2). The calculated gravimetric specific capacitance of NCNG electroactive material is about 30, 26.5, 23, 20 and 18.5 F/g at the applied current densities of 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 A g−1, respectively (Fig. 8(c)). Moreover, based on the gravimetric specific capacitance the energy densities and power densities are computed using the Eqs. (3) and (4), the respective results are showed in Ragone plots (Fig. 8(d)). The calculated energy densities of the synthesized NCNG electroactive material are 9.4, 8.3, 7.2, 6.2 and 5.7 Wh kg−1 at the power densities of 1542, 2989, 4480, 5952 and 7462 W kg−1, respectively. The obtained results are better than that of reported values in two electrode symmetrical carbon-based systems [56–60]. Therefore, the two-electrode studies revealed that the synthesized NCNG electroactive material can be applied as a symmetric electrode for supercapacitor devices. These excellent electrochemical properties of the synthesized NCNG electroactive material is due to the novel grass-like morphology with porous structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological study of the fabricated working electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Morphology of the fabricated working electrode (NCNG electroactive material) was analyzed before and after the electrochemical performance to determine the structural stability of the synthesized NCNG electroactive material. Fig. 9 displays the FESEM images of the NCNG electroactive material coated carbon cloth before and after all of the electrochemical measurements including the CV, GCD, and EIS. The FESEM images of NCNG electroactive material after the electrochemical measurements (Fig. 9(d–f) and (g–i)) are similar to that of the synthesized NCNG electroactive material before electrochemical measurements (Fig. 9(a–c)). The images reveal the electroactive materials are finely coated on the carbon cloth. The grass-like morphology also clear and the structure retains even after the electrochemical measurements is clear from the inset of Fig. 9(f) and (i). The results conclude that the synthesized NCNG electroactive material is electrochemically stable in 1 M H2SO4. Hence, the synthesized NCNG electroactive material is suitable for supercapacitors.

In summary, a novel mesoporous NCNG was synthesized from used baby-diaper absorbent through simple carbonization under the argon atmosphere and their electrochemical performance toward the supercapacitor was investigated. First, the synthesized NCNG was thoroughly characterized by various analytical techniques. All of the analytical results strongly suggest that the synthesized NCNG having significant amount of oxygen and nitrogen owing to the functionalities in the graphitic structure and multiple graphitic carbon layers with mesoporous nature. On the basis of the novel morphology with porosity and a reasonable degree of graphitization, the synthesized NCNG was used as an electroactive material for the flexible supercapacitors. The synthesized NCNG electroactive material delivered a maximum specific capacitance of 81 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 with excellent capacitance retention of 95% even after 10,000 cycles of GCD. Further, EIS of NCNG electroactive materials showed the enhancement of electrochemical properties including capacitive behaviour and electrochemical stability. To the best of the author knows, this is the first report on the synthesis of novel NCNG from used baby-diaper absorbent and applied as an electroactive material for the flexible supercapacitors. The excellent electrochemical properties of the NCNG is due to the novel grass-like morphology with porous structure. Importantly, the garbage-wasted used baby-diaper has turned into electroactive material for energy storage devices that is clean, renewable and environmentally-friendly energy conversion.
Instrumentation methods, FESEM images, XRD patterns, FTIR spectroscopy analysis, electrochemical measurements including CV, GCD, and EIS of the synthesized NCNG.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea grant funded by the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (MSIT) of Korea government (2017R1C1B5076345). Also, the author greatly acknowledge the National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (2012M3A7B4049675) and the Priority Research Centers Program (2014R1A6A1031189).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2019.06.020.
